sentences.text: Yes.

What I want to mention-- maybe it's not clear-- that I was in Ravensbruck Camp a short time, but I was sent in Ravensbruck working camp because-- I presume because I was a live prisoner of concentration camp, they sent me to work.
And that where is the most of the time up to my liberation, I was in working camp, which is sent by Ravensbruck Camp.
I wouldn't know exactly how close.
It might be not that close because I remember that was big, big factories [POINTING] on a place that nothing around it, just factories, you know.
The man factories was a few kilometers from women, because where I was working was only woman's working camp.
There was men's, which was a few kilometers from there.
And nothing was around us.
The only surround us a very high voltage of wire, and that's that.
We were every morning escorted by SS men to the hall where we work and escorted back to where, where, quarter where we sleep, stay.
So that this period was up to my liberation, working underground, having this appells, and this staying in the snow.
And all those nightmares was in working camp.
So we were working day, and we were, we were working night, because they can take us at night and put us outside to stay while-- when they feel like us not to rest.
That's it.
What were the sanitary facilities like?
Yes.
Didn't I tell you that we-- our leisure time was spent knocking the lice.

In evening, when we didn't go for appell outside, we stayed home after supper, that coffee and piece of bread which we left in the morning half, and at night half, because we were hungry by the time we come home back from work.
So we took our clothes, and we had a lot of lice.
We have to knock them, because they didn't give us nothing to kill them.
So what we did, we took the clothes out, put them on inside out and looking for those lice and knocking them with the nails.
Was dysentery a problem?
Oh, [SIGHS] definitely.
They didn't have nothing.
That okay if you get some sickness or this, they can take us and burn.
It's it's it was out of question.
You have to be healthy.
So we just have to help ourselves, whatever we can do.
We have only one change of the clothes, so if you want to be clean, we could do it only at night when we come.
We took out-- we knock this, all the lice, and then we wash it, but we have to be prepared and have, have wet clothes to go to work.
And when we work, of course, by the time we finish work, it's dry.
But half wet it was always if you want to wash it.
But sometimes you come so moral and physically out that we couldn't.
We were happy to just hit your head to the cushion and sleep.
Whatever-- we had only about five, six hours sleep.
By the time we come home-- we finish 8 o'clock.
By the time you come home, about 9:00.
By the time we have coffee, about 10:00. 10:00 to 4 o'clock, that's it.
By 5 o'clock, we out, because an hour was walking to the factory where we were working.
So it was a very hardship, so sometimes we didn't feel to wash it and go have particularly in winter.
So we have just knock them and see-- and hope for the best.
Of course, we got a lot of casualties from sickness, and that's where I come, because my friend was in, in this-- she was a sister and revered.
That's how we call it in the factory.
We had a-- how you-- sanitary [? quarter. ?] If you have somebody sick, I rang her, and she was making sure that she get a place in the hospital, because we-- the hospital was where we lived.
On the factory, it somebody sort of have some sort of cut or bruise or something, so it was done by the factory sanitary sister.
But hospital was in the quarters where we lived, special block.
That's it.
We start-- by about April, late of April, there-- we knew that we will be getting away from there because Russians start to be very close, and they didn't want Russian to come to this factory.
So what they decided to [? recreate ?] the whole camp from there up north.
And we-- they start to prepare the whole camp to walk out to north Rostock.
I remember, Rostock.
It was north, much north, from the, from the place that we had that's working camp.
And it's happened.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
Oh, boy, the march.
So, I think it's in April, because by Seder of 1945, I ran away from the walk.
The walk was prepared by [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a lot of SS men, women and men, a lot of dogs.
All was very heavily [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] armed.
They had all sort of guns and those rifles and all automatics, all sort of things, and a lot of them.
So they put us in the middle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From one side, the other side, in the front, in the back, SS men was sort of heading us-- that was north Rostock.
We started to walk.
We walk a long-- we walk.
They didn't give us no transportation at all.
We just have to walk wherever we could.
You know, I don't even remember having food or water.
Oh, no, we had the water, because I-- they give us-- I can't remember.
I can't remember at all food.
We just were walking.
Most probably die.
They had a feeling that we won't walk long.
I don't know because I run.
No houses.
I wouldn't say forest, like forest.
It was, it was a road, a big road that-- one side was [? slick ?] down, and further up was little forest with bushes.
Very small, but not high.
And the other side was the same thing.
And we were walking through the road.
That much I remember.
Oh, they wanted to go quick.
Schnell, schnell, schnell, schnell.
And of course, when you, when you start, you can go schnell, but when you walk a few hours or day, so you can't go schnell.

So they-- of course, the, the people who was behind was the hardest time because they could stick you with their gun or whatever they've got and make you [? shoo. ?] A lot of them couldn't make it, so people have to help each other to, to walk.
It was very bad, very bad.
I remember the walk-- oh, I remember that we stopped somewhere.
That's where I lost my diary.
I had a diary, and I lost the diary.
We stopped in a little forest, but I suppose that there was a picnic ground, because I remember there was tables, and was-- how you call it?
Bench?
Benches.
We could sit down.
But the most of us was sitting on-- assume we got permission, we sit wherever we stay, because-- but I, I could see there, and that's where I tried to find my diary.
And I couldn't find it, and I realized it's just before that picnic that I lost it.
I lost it.
And of course, whoever found it, he's lucky.
But I am very unfortunate, because there was a lot of names that I wanted to remember.
Because there was one officer that I hate her with all my heart.
I would have kill her if I could.
Elsa.
I think it's Elsa, but I can't be sure.
Oh!
She was horrible.
And you see, the names I forget very quick unless it's something that I never put down and I have to remember, like the people who helped me to survive, my family and me.
I remember until now.
Numbers I remember easy, but the names.
You see, when I was liberated, I couldn't remember my name of my father and mother.
I couldn't sleep all night.
[SOBBING] I, I was thinking how could I forget my parents?
I couldn't.
Names just gone.
Gone.
It took me a lot of time, a few good days, might be weeks, till I remember how my father and mother.
So--
No, no, no, no.
No.
It was only the road we saw.
They make sure that we don't go somewhere where we will be helped or somewhere that we could see anything, so it was the road.
Just-- I think it was the road for cars or for any transportation [INAUDIBLE].
Might be army.
Their army can go, but no people.
There are no buildings around.
Just a road.
[SIGHS] I think it was a day or maybe two.
I know that on the third day I, I decided to run.
What I did-- I, I had enough.
I thought I won't be able to walk, and I knew what is going to, to be if I don't be able to walk.
I started to go back, more back and back.
I couldn't follow.
And that's when my mind start to work what I have to do, because I couldn't walk more.
So, I didn't look where to run.
I just concentrate how to run.
And I thought that if I go more back and do something, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe it will be less visible.
So in one [INAUDIBLE], I saw little bushes.
Not much, two or three.
And I thought if I just jump this road and put myself down there, I might be able.
And I saw nobody around.
I thought that's a good point, and I just lay down and got down to lie.
How long I could lie?
And my, my mistake was that I got up.
I might have-- they might have not seen if I would have lied there till they go all through.
But I couldn't lie, and I got up.
And as soon I got up and start to run, I heard, halt.
And the one SS man come to me with, of course, rifle, and said to lie down.
I lie down, and I said, you know, goodbye Charlie.
That's it.
That's my end.
I was sure he's going to shoot me.
What do you think he did?
He said, get up and go back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got up and went back.
Then what happened?
And they put me straight in front to go back, and I went about, about two or three hours more, but I was clever then.
I said I will never get up.
I just go in this ditch, and I stayed there.
And that's what I did.
They didn't never found me.
They went [? out, ?] but when it's dark-- I stayed there till dark.
When I got up, I saw the others got up.
This was the way I met another four girls which ran with me.
I didn't know them.
I didn't know they're going run.
I just decided that's the time.
I don't care.
They shoot, let them shoot.
But I'm not going to [INAUDIBLE].
But I knew what to do, because while I was lying, nobody even knew.
It's when I get up and run, that's when they [INAUDIBLE].
It was too many of them, too many people.
They have to look for this and that, so this time they never got me.
And that was about 1st of May.
When I saw them, we were all together, start to walk through these bushes, to this forest.
And we walked and walked, and then we saw a little shape.
I didn't know what it-- but it was not far.
When I-- we walked a long time, we saw further up some sort of building.
We didn't want to go there.
We were frightened.
But this was forest, and there's a cabin.
And there was a window broken.
We look through the window and open the door.
Door was open, and the window was broken.
We went to the-- nobody's there, so we decided, all of us, we stay in this.
So we went in and stayed there.
And we stayed there, and everybody was shaking like mad.
We didn't have nothing to eat, of course.
We didn't have nothing to-- but we were happy.
We were free.
Who will-- well, whoever will come, we're going to stay here.
We stayed there a whole night, a whole day, and a night.
In the morning, next morning, we heard tanks.
We didn't know what tanks.
It could be German tanks.
So we didn't know.
But as soon we heard, nobody wants to move, so I move.
I have to be fast.
I move and run to the road.
And all of a sudden, I heard Red Army.
So I couldn't even say Red Army.
I said-- you know, they had all those [INAUDIBLE] and had red, red stars.
So I start to scream red stars, red stars.
[INAUDIBLE] hearing me.
I screaming, but nobody comes out because I don't scream Red Army.
Red star, red star, and they still don't know what is that.
And I-- then I ran-- I, I, I feel as I have to tell-- I ran to them.
I said, look, I think it's the Red Army.
Come!
And we went run, and we stay on the road, all of us uniform, of course.
And we stayed there-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[LAUGHS].
Can you--
About 5th of May.
It was in the morning that we heard tank coming in.
We run down there.
We stay, and we cry.
Scream and cry, scream and cry.
And one of the from the tank-- an officer or what, I don't remember-- but he jumped out and rushed to me and took his hat and put on mine.
And I, I had it even now, this hat, because it's very-- I was happy.
We were crying, and they start to-- don't cry, don't cry.
We come, you free, all sort of things.
So that was the happiest day of my life and the day that I was waiting for three years.
And, and of course, they straight away wanted to help us.
The tanks went, and then after them come officers in, in cars.
And one of the captain officer come and said to us, stop crying, sit-- go to the car.
We take you to the house, and you take whatever you wanted.
Dress yourself, take this thing off.
You look horrible [INAUDIBLE].
And really, they did.
They took us in the car, and they start to go and, and ask which villa you want?
And we say, for us, it was no different.
Wherever we go, we wanted to wash.
We want to be able to sit down properly in there.
So they took us to one villa-- it was two-story house-- and, and said, you can stay here, whatever you want.
Whatever it is here, it's all yours.

And of course, [INAUDIBLE] I was all the time I was in concentration camp, my dream was when they free me, the first thing I wanted is a big piece of bread, and that's what will be the top of my dreams come true.
And when they free me, and they said, we have in this villa-- downstairs it was a cellar.
All sort of things there were.
You see, Germans, they like to do all jams and all those things, and everything was there, bread and dry bread and all.
And they said, oh, it's all yours.
Take it.
Take it.
And I sat like a dumdum.
I couldn't move.
I didn't want to take nothing to eat.
I couldn't.
I couldn't even make myself to get up and take something.
Absolutely.
Till now, I don't know why.
I just lost all my appetite.
All my dream to eat a piece of bread went, disappeared.
I didn't.
But not all were like me.
Some of them otherwise start to look for some food to eat, and apparently, they got very sick by night.
By night-- I didn't even eat at night.
Only next morning when the officer come and ask me have you eaten?
I said no.
So he said, why, why?
And then I remember, I said, I will have a cup of tea or coffee, whatever is there, and I took a little piece of bread.
That was my-- in the whole day and night, I couldn't eat.
But I had very bad experience with the other girls.
They were very sick.
Of course, they have help.
There was a medical center where you went, and they help and come give them some tablets, but I was lucky.
I didn't think of it as that can hurt me.
I just couldn't eat.
For all excitement, all this things, I couldn't eat.
I start only think to go home and see my, my sister, see my parents.
And the first thing I said to, to my-- to officer that did all that.
I said, when I go home?
Oh, oh, wait.
Wait a minute.
Not yet, not yet.
Where were you?
Do you know where you were?
That was near Dusseldorf, I think, there where it was.
And he said, not now, not now, and I was very disappointed.
It knocked me down.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he said, you will be home, you will be home whenever.
And of course, what they did, they said, we need you here.
You know German.
We need in special-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you call the special thing in army that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] agents, special.
Service, yes, special service.
That they have to call Germans and ask all sort of things.
And by the time the war-- the war was finished 9th of May, so by the time the war finished, I was already established as a, as, as a interpreter, German interpreter, in the special service.
Secret Service, special Secret Service.
And of course, they, they need me.
I could see that they need me, and they said, we need you.
You can't go home.
And I scream I want to go home.
You can't go home.
So I was working with the Secret Service for a long time.
As interpreter only?
As interpreter.
Yes, as interpreter.
They give me a special [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you call, a document, that I can go to all Russian-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Russian--
Yes, that's right.
And who I am, what I'm doing, all sort of things, and I start there.

When-- that was the whole May, June, July, August, September Now in August-- in August after the war, they decided that there was a lot of Russian troops on the German territory, and the, the war is over, so they need some entertainment.
They knew that I was in army ensemble song and dances, so they knew that I play accordion.
They knew that I play piano.

So because, you know, when they free me, before they put me in a Secret Service [INAUDIBLE] they have to ask all sort of who my father and who my mother and what I did and who I am to make sure that I was in concentration.
They knew.
They free me.
But that's things they have to know.
So then they put me in the Secret Service, but when they find out I do this and that, they decided to put me on the head of concert group which will go in all army places to entertain.
So I have a group of 22 people, orchestra, people-- dancers, singers, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] this-- the people that can-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] poem to read.
Recite poetry.
Recital, yes.
I have soloist pianist.
I have a soloist-- how you call them, violinist, soloist of trombone, all sorts of things.
So I put the group together, and we start to go around this.
And there was a few groups around, and when it was-- they had a competition, so we went on competition.
[INAUDIBLE] people, people from theater because we start establish in there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all sort of things for Russian.
So there was--
Already in Poland.
In Poland.
In Poland, in Liegnitz there was a drama theater.
Then in [INAUDIBLE] there was a drama theater.
In Liegnitz was a music hall.
So what they-- on this, they find out that I should go to the theater.
They send me in drama, so in [NON-ENGLISH], that's Poland, I was working in theater, drama theater.
Then they knew that I dance and, and play and sing.
They took me in musical, musical comedy, so send me in Liegnitz.
I was working musical comedy.
OK?
This is the end of the tape.
OK.
